name: Caro-Kann Defense
description: A solid defense with c6. Black builds a strong pawn structure.

moves.commentary: The Caro-Kann - a solid response that prepares d5.

children.commentary: d4 stakes a claim in the center. The main response to the Caro-Kann.
Nc3 develops and increases pressure on d5.
The Classical Caro-Kann. Black takes and will develop the bishop.
Nxe4 recaptures. The knight is well placed in the center.
Bf5 develops the bishop to an active square.
Ng3 attacks the bishop and gains time.

tree.commentary: The Classical - Black takes and will develop.

strategy.keyIdea: Support the d5 pawn challenge with ...c6 before playing it, achieving a solid central presence without the bad bishop problem of the French.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After the opening, Black's light-squared bishop is free to develop outside the pawn chain — a key advantage over the French Defense. Look to exchange the light-squared bishops if possible. Black's plans often involve queenside play with ...c5 or centralizing the knight on d5.
strategy.watchOut: Falling into passive, cramped positions — the Caro-Kann should be solid but not inert, and Black needs to create active counterplay.
typicalGoals: Solve the light-squared bishop before it becomes trapped
Aim for ...c5 to challenge White's center
Centralize the knight on d5 if the opportunity arises
Keep the pawn structure intact and avoid unnecessary weaknesses

quiz.question: You're playing the Caro-Kann. What's the main structural advantage over the French Defense?

options.text: You have more space on the queenside
options.explanation: Space distribution is similar between the Caro-Kann and French — the key difference is about bishop activity, not space.

options.text: Your light-squared bishop isn't locked behind your pawns
options.explanation: Correct. In the French, the light-squared bishop is often permanently bad. In the Caro-Kann, ...c6 before ...d5 means the bishop can come out to f5 or g4.

options.text: You can attack on the kingside more easily
options.explanation: The Caro-Kann is a positional, solid opening — Black typically looks for queenside counterplay, not kingside attacks.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

c3.opponentMoveSummary: White played c3, preparing to advance e5 and gain space in the center (Advance Variation setup).
c3.whyBookBreaks: The Caro-Kann main lines assume White plays d4 and Nc3 or Nd2. c3 prepares the Advance with e5, closing the center.
c3.opponentStrategy: Advance Variation setup — White gains space with e5, closes the center, and aims to attack with f4-f5.
forwardGuidance: Complete development; the Advance often leads to a closed, strategic battle.
Consider ...c5 or ...Bf5 to create counterplay; White's space can be challenged.

Nc3.opponentMoveSummary: White played Nc3, developing the knight and heading for the Classical or Fantasy Variation.
Nc3.whyBookBreaks: The main lines assume d4 and Nd2 or a different move order. Nc3 leads to sharper lines where White may sacrifice a pawn.
Nc3.opponentStrategy: Classical or Fantasy Variation — White develops actively; Nc3 can lead to sharp tactics and pawn sacrifices.
forwardGuidance: Be alert for tactics; the Nc3 lines can be sharp.
Complete development and castle; White's aggression can be met with solid play.

exd5.opponentMoveSummary: White played exd5, exchanging the central pawns and simplifying to a symmetrical structure.
exd5.whyBookBreaks: The main lines assume White maintains the tension with Nc3 or Nd2. exd5 removes the central tension immediately.
exd5.opponentStrategy: Exchange Variation — White simplifies to a symmetrical pawn structure and aims for a slight endgame advantage.
forwardGuidance: Recapture with ...cxd5; the position is roughly equal.
Complete development; the Exchange often leads to a quiet, technical game.
